name: Bob Wills
abstract: James Robert Wills (March 6, 1905 – May 13, 1975) was an American musician, songwriter, and bandleader. Considered by music authorities as the founder of Western swing, he was known widely as the King of Western Swing (although Spade Cooley self-promoted the moniker "King of Western Swing" from 1942 to 1969).

about_quotes.speaker: Dick Spottswood
about_quotes.evidence: I mean isn’t Bob Wills a pretty good blues singer? ... If you listen to some of Bob Wills’ records... they’re out swinging a lot of those big bands
about_quotes.doc_title: Dick Spottswood interviewed by Monk Rowe, via Zoom, February 13, 2023

about_quotes.speaker: Rebecca Kilgore
about_quotes.evidence: Western swing which is a music originated by Bob Wills... I was in a really fun big band that emulated that Bob Wills style.
about_quotes.doc_title: Rebecca Kilgore interviewed by Monk Rowe, Chautauqua, New York, September 13, 1997

about_quotes.speaker: John "Knocky" Parker
about_quotes.evidence: The interviewee discusses Bob Wills' band structure, payroll, and soloists in comparison to his own group (the Doughboys), but never states he played in Wills' band or had a personal interaction with Wills.

about_quotes.speaker: Tim Kelly
about_quotes.evidence: I, I just really did not, didn't run into them. Yeah. They came from, I guess, about 1926 on, until now. But I watched the development of them, been very interested in them.

about_quotes.speaker: Claude Williams
about_quotes.evidence: He was at Play Boy Flour for a long time... And that was all completely different from what Bob Wills was playing.
about_quotes.doc_title: Claude Williams interviewed by Michael Woods, Los Angeles, California, September 4, 1995

about_quotes.speaker: Chico Hamilton
about_quotes.evidence: [INTERVIEWER] Not Bob Wills, not the Texas Playboys? [INTERVIEWEE] No.
about_quotes.doc_title: Chico Hamilton — Smithsonian Jazz Oral History
